name: Manes
description: The Manes (Latin: Manes, plural) represent the spirits of the deceased in Roman religion. They are often conceived as ancestral spirits, benevolent and protective of their descendants. Unlike the malevolent Lemures, who were also spirits of the dead, the Manes were generally honored and propitiated. Veneration of the Manes was a crucial aspect of Roman family and state religion, emphasizing the importance of honoring ancestors and maintaining a connection with the past. Their worship typically involved offerings of food, wine, and flowers at tombs or household shrines, especially during the Parentalia festival in February, a time dedicated to honoring deceased family members. The Manes were considered to dwell in the Underworld, a realm ruled by Pluto and Proserpina, but they maintained a connection to the living world through ritual and remembrance. Failure to properly honor the Manes could result in misfortune or divine displeasure, highlighting the reciprocal relationship between the living and the dead in Roman belief. Maintaining the 'pax deorum' (peace of the gods) included ensuring the Manes were content.
subtitle: Spirits of the Dead

keyMyths.title: Parentalia Festival
keyMyths.description: The Parentalia was an annual Roman festival held from February 13th to 21st, dedicated to honoring the Manes. During this time, Romans would visit the tombs of their ancestors, leaving offerings of food, wine, and flowers. Businesses were often closed, and marriages were forbidden, as the festival was considered a solemn period of remembrance. Neglecting the Parentalia was believed to anger the Manes and bring misfortune upon the family. It highlighted the importance of maintaining a connection with the deceased and ensuring their continued well-being in the afterlife.

keyMyths.title: Violation of Tombs
keyMyths.description: Disturbing or desecrating a tomb was considered a grave offense in Roman society, as it was seen as a direct insult to the Manes residing within. Such actions were believed to provoke the wrath of the spirits, resulting in curses and misfortune for the perpetrator and their family. Laws were in place to protect tombs from vandalism and theft, reflecting the deep respect and reverence for the deceased and their final resting place. The sanctity of tombs was crucial for maintaining the 'pax deorum' and ensuring the continued well-being of the community.

keyMyths.title: Genius and Manes
keyMyths.description: Every Roman male was thought to possess a 'genius,' a personal tutelary spirit. Upon death, the genius was believed to merge with the collective Manes. This transition marked the individual's integration into the broader realm of ancestral spirits, where they continued to watch over and influence their descendants. The genius represented the life force and individuality of a person, while the Manes represented their connection to the family lineage and the afterlife. This blending of individual and collective spirits underscored the importance of family and ancestry in Roman religious beliefs.
